canto_title: The Ascent to a Higher Sphere and the Vision of the New Light

scenes.scene_name: The Proem and the Analogy of Thomas
scenes.summary: Dante introduces a metaphor regarding the movement of water and links it to the intellectual legacy of St. Thomas Aquinas and Beatrice.
An unnamed voice asks whether the light that nourishes the souls in the Empyrean will remain with them eternally in their glorified bodies.
The saints react to the question with a collective, joyful movement and song, similar to dancers in a circle.
One soul expresses a brief lament for the loss of earthly life, though it is quickly eclipsed by the heavenly refreshment.
The souls sing a hymn to the Holy Trinity, celebrating the unity and complexity of the Three-in-One.
A modest voice, perhaps that of an angel to Mary, responds that their love will expand to match the extent of the heavenly feast.
The voice explains how light, ardor, and vision increase in a reciprocal cycle of growth and illumination.
The speaker uses the metaphor of a coal that produces a flame to explain how the glorified flesh will handle the intense light.
The choirs cry 'Amen' so swiftly that it reveals their intense longing for their departed loved ones.
A new, even brighter light appears, and Dante perceives new celestial bodies beginning to orbit outside the previous spheres.

scenes.scene_name: The Intensity of the Holy Spirit
scenes.summary: Dante struggles to endure the sudden, blinding brilliance of the light associated with the Holy Spirit.
Amidst the overwhelming visions, the beauty of Beatrice shines so brightly that Dante's mind cannot follow.
Dante's vision recovers, and he realizes he has been moved to a higher, more exalted state alongside Beatrice.
Dante notices that the star guiding him appears more intensely red than before, signaling his ascent.
With all his heart, Dante offers a prayer of total consecration to God.
Dante experiences such intense splendor within two rays of light that he compares the sight to the glory of Helios.

scenes.scene_name: The Analogy of the Milky Way
scenes.summary: The poet compares the scattered lights in the heavens to the way the Milky Way appears across the sky.
Dante witnesses a light in the shape of a cross representing Christ, a vision so profound he lacks a worthy earthly comparison.
He describes the complex, flickering, and varying movements of the celestial lights, comparing them to optical shadows.
The movement of the lights creates a beautiful, wordless melody, much like the sound of a harp or lyre.
Dante hears messages of 'Rise' and 'Conquer' within the music, though he cannot understand the specific words.
Dante confesses his intense enamoredness with the vision and defends his poetic difficulty by stating that true spiritual joy is best experienced rather than described.
